name: hig-components-dialogs
description: Apple HIG guidance for presentation components including alerts, action sheets, popovers, sheets, and digit entry views. Use this skill when the user says 'should I use an alert or a sheet,' 'how do I show a confirmation dialog,' 'when should I use a popover,' 'my modals are annoying users,' or asks about alert design, action sheet, popover, sheet, modal, dialog, digit entry, confirmation dialog, warning dialog, modal presentation, non-modal content, destructive action confirmation, or overlay UI patterns. Cross-references: hig-components-menus, hig-components-controls, hig-components-search, hig-patterns.
capabilities: Recommend alert vs sheet vs action sheet based on use case
Provide button ordering and labeling guidelines per Apple HIG
Explain dismiss behavior for each presentation type
Show platform-specific differences (iPhone vs iPad vs Mac vs watchOS)
Suggest alternatives to modals (inline feedback, undo)
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All static findings are false positives. The skill contains Apple's Human Interface Guidelines documentation for UI presentation components. The flagged patterns (backticks, URLs, generic English words) are legitimate documentation elements, not security threats.
valueStatement: Get Apple Human Interface Guidelines for alerts, sheets, popovers, and action sheets. Choose the right modal pattern for each platform and avoid common UX mistakes with user-interrupting dialogs.
limitations: Does not provide working code examples
Does not cover Android or Web platform guidelines
Focused only on presentation components, not other HIG areas

useCases.targetUser: Mobile app developer building iOS apps
useCases.title: Choosing confirmation dialog type
useCases.description: User wants to confirm a destructive action (delete, erase, remove). Skill recommends alert vs action sheet based on platform and provides button styling guidance.

useCases.targetUser: Cross-platform developer working on Apple platforms
useCases.title: Modal presentation decision
useCases.description: User asks 'should I use a sheet or a popover?' Skill explains when sheets maintain context better and when popovers work on iPad/Mac.

useCases.title: Reducing alert fatigue
useCases.description: User complains about too many modals in their app. Skill suggests inline alternatives, undo patterns, and when to avoid interrupting the user.

promptTemplates.title: Basic component guidance
promptTemplates.prompt: Should I use an alert or an action sheet for [use case]?
promptTemplates.scenario: Choosing between alert and action sheet for a specific interaction

promptTemplates.title: Platform-specific behavior
promptTemplates.prompt: How does [component] behave differently on iPhone vs iPad?
promptTemplates.scenario: Understanding platform variations for modal presentations

promptTemplates.title: Button and text guidelines
promptTemplates.prompt: What are the best practices for alert button labels and ordering?
promptTemplates.scenario: Writing clear, actionable button text for dialogs

promptTemplates.title: Alternative patterns
promptTemplates.prompt: What are alternatives to using a modal for [scenario]?
promptTemplates.scenario: Reducing modal usage and improving user flow

bestPractices: Use alerts sparingly and only for critical situations requiring immediate attention
Provide specific, actionable button labels (Delete, Save) instead of generic OK/Cancel
Always include a Cancel button for destructive action confirmations
antiPatterns: Using alerts for informative messages that could be shown inline
Placing destructive actions as the default button
Using the same component for all modal scenarios without considering alternatives

faq.question: When should I use an alert vs a sheet?
faq.answer: Use alerts for critical interruptions requiring immediate response. Use sheets for focused tasks where maintaining context with the underlying content is helpful.

faq.question: Can I use the same design across iPhone and iPad?
faq.answer: Not always. Action sheets appear as popovers on iPad. Sheets adapt differently. Always test on target platforms.

faq.question: How many buttons should an alert have?
faq.answer: Up to three buttons maximum. Use one button for simple acknowledgment, two for confirm/cancel, three when a destructive option exists.

faq.question: What is the cancel button placement?
faq.answer: Place Cancel at the bottom of action sheets (iOS) or leading side (macOS). On watchOS, place in upper-left corner.

faq.question: Should I use OK or specific button labels?
faq.answer: Always prefer specific labels like Delete, Save, or Confirm. Use OK only for purely informational alerts.

faq.question: How do I make modals accessible?
faq.answer: Ensure voiceover announces the title, message, and all button options. Support keyboard navigation for macOS modals.
